content.text: Selecting Among Tools

When multiple tools are defined, the model reads every description and picks the one whose 'when to use' clause best matches the user's intent. Understanding how selection works lets you write descriptions that produce consistent, predictable choices — and diagnose why the wrong tool was picked when selection fails.

attrs.title: Model reads descriptions and selects the matching tool

content.text: Selection is description matching, not capability matching.

The model cannot inspect what a tool does — it only reads what you wrote. Two tools with identical capabilities but different descriptions will be selected in different situations. Write descriptions for the model, not for human readers.
Observing Selection Behaviour
The simplest way to understand selection is to run test messages and observe which tool each message triggers:
User intent
Correct tool
Why
Factual question about policy
search_documents
Description says 'Use for factual questions'
Bug report
create_ticket
Description says 'Use when user reports a bug'
Email request without confirmation
None (text reply)
Description says 'Use only after confirmation'
Disambiguating Overlapping Tools
When two tools serve related goals, description must include explicit trigger conditions and, where helpful, explicit exclusions. Vague descriptions cause random selection:

# When two tools serve overlapping purposes, description must disambiguate.

# BAD — model cannot decide between them
bad_tools = [
    {"name": "search", "description": "Searches for things.", ...},
    {"name": "lookup", "description": "Looks things up.", ...},
]

# GOOD — explicit trigger conditions for each
good_tools = [
    {
        "name": "search_knowledge_base",
        "description": (
            "Search internal documentation: FAQs, policies, product specs, procedures. "
            "Use for questions the support agent should know from company documentation."
        ),
        ...
    },
    {
        "name": "search_web",
        "description": (
            "Search the public internet for information not in the knowledge base. "
            "Use when the user asks about external topics, competitors, or current events. "
            "Do NOT use for internal company information — prefer search_knowledge_base."
        ),
        ...
    }
]

# The key phrases:
# "Use for X" — positive trigger
# "Do NOT use for Y" — negative trigger (explicit exclusion)
# "Use this instead of Z when..." — comparative disambiguation
Use 'Use for X' (positive trigger) to state when to call the tool
Use 'Do NOT use for Y' (negative trigger) to prevent false positives
Use 'Use this instead of Z when...' for direct comparison between similar tools
Avoid describing the implementation — describe the user intent that should trigger the tool
Tool Order and Robustness
Well-written descriptions should produce the same selection regardless of where the tool appears in the list. If tool order matters, your descriptions are too ambiguous:

# Experiment: does tool order in the list affect selection?

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Base tools
search_tool = {
    "name": "search_documents",
    "description": "Search the knowledge base for factual questions.",
    "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["query"]}
}
ticket_tool = {
    "name": "create_ticket",
    "description": "Create a ticket for bugs and feature requests.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"title": {"type": "string"}, "body": {"type": "string"}},
        "required": ["title", "body"]
    }
}

msg = "How do I export my data?"

# Order A: search first
r_a = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=256,
    tools=[search_tool, ticket_tool],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": msg}]
)

# Order B: ticket first
r_b = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=256,
    tools=[ticket_tool, search_tool],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": msg}]
)

print("Order A:", [b.name for b in r_a.content if b.type == "tool_use"])
print("Order B:", [b.name for b in r_b.content if b.type == "tool_use"])
# Both should → search_documents (good descriptions are robust to ordering)
# If you see different results, the descriptions are too ambiguous.

In practice, the model is generally insensitive to tool order when descriptions are clear. If you observe order sensitivity, rewrite the descriptions to make each tool's trigger condition unambiguous.
System Prompt Selection Policies

For production agents, combine tool descriptions with explicit selection rules in the system prompt. System-prompt policies override the model's default reasoning and produce consistent behaviour across different user phrasings:

# System prompt policies give the model explicit rules for tool selection
# that override its default judgement.

system = """You are a customer support agent for Acme Corp.

Tool selection rules:
1. ALWAYS call search_knowledge_base before answering any question about
   products, pricing, returns, or shipping — do not answer from memory.
2. Call create_ticket when the user mentions a bug or explicitly requests
   follow-up. Do not call it for questions you can answer immediately.
3. NEVER call send_email unless the user explicitly says "yes, send it" or
   "please email me". Ask for confirmation if unsure.
4. If a question cannot be answered by search_knowledge_base results, say so
   honestly — do not guess.
"""

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=system,
    tools=tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What are your shipping rates?"}]
)
# Model calls search_knowledge_base because rule 1 mandates it,
# even if it "knows" a generic answer.

Four effective policy patterns: ALWAYS call X for topic Y (mandatory coverage), NEVER call Z without condition C (safety gate), prefer A over B for context D (disambiguation), and if results are insufficient do E (fallback).
Dynamic Tool Lists
Passing only context-relevant tools reduces selection noise and latency. Build a function that returns a filtered tool list based on what has appeared in the conversation:

# Serve only tools relevant to the current conversation state.
# Fewer tools = faster selection, less chance of wrong picks.

def get_tools_for_context(messages: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Return a filtered tools list based on what has happened so far."""
    # Check if user has reported a bug
    user_text = " ".join(
        m["content"] for m in messages
        if m["role"] == "user" and isinstance(m["content"], str)
    ).lower()

    available = [search_tool]   # always available

    bug_keywords = ["bug", "error", "crash", "broken", "doesn't work", "fails"]
    if any(k in user_text for k in bug_keywords):
        available.append(ticket_tool)

    if "email" in user_text or "send" in user_text:
        available.append(email_tool)

    return available

# Each turn gets a context-appropriate tools list
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=get_tools_for_context(messages),
    messages=messages
)
Start with the minimum tool set for the initial turn (often just search/read tools)
Add write tools only after the conversation establishes that a write action is needed
Gate destructive tools (delete, send) behind explicit user confirmation before adding them to the list
Removing a tool mid-conversation is invisible to the model — it will not notice it is gone
Testing Selection Accuracy
Build a test harness that runs known messages and checks whether the expected tools were called. This lets you measure accuracy and catch regressions when you revise descriptions:

# Build a small test harness to measure selection accuracy across your tool set

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class SelectionTestCase:
    message: str
    expected_tools: list[str]   # empty list = no tool call expected
    must_not_call: list[str] = None

def run_selection_tests(test_cases: list[SelectionTestCase], tools: list[dict]) -> dict:
    passed = 0
    failed = []

    for case in test_cases:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=256, tools=tools,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case.message}]
        )
        called = [b.name for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]

        # Check expected tools all called
        missing = [e for e in case.expected_tools if e not in called]
        # Check forbidden tools not called
        forbidden = [b for b in called if case.must_not_call and b in case.must_not_call]

        if not missing and not forbidden:
            passed += 1
        else:
            failed.append({
                "message": case.message,
                "expected": case.expected_tools,
                "actual": called,
                "missing": missing,
                "forbidden": forbidden
            })

    return {
        "passed": passed,
        "failed": len(failed),
        "total": len(test_cases),
        "accuracy": passed / len(test_cases),
        "failures": failed
    }

# Define test cases
test_cases = [
    SelectionTestCase("What is your refund policy?",       ["search_documents"]),
    SelectionTestCase("I found a bug in login",            ["create_ticket"]),
    SelectionTestCase("Hello, how are you?",               []),
    SelectionTestCase("Send me an email summary",          [], must_not_call=["send_email"]),
    SelectionTestCase("How do I cancel my subscription?",  ["search_documents"]),
]

results = run_selection_tests(test_cases, tools)
print(f"Accuracy: {results['accuracy']:.0%}  ({results['passed']}/{results['total']})")
for f in results["failures"]:
    print(f"  FAIL: {f['message']!r}")
    print(f"    expected={f['expected']}  actual={f['actual']}")

Run these tests in CI whenever you change a tool description. A drop in accuracy signals that a description revision broke a previously working trigger. Aim for ≥ 95% accuracy on your test suite before deploying to production.
Fallback When No Tool Is Called
When you expect a tool to be called but the model answers in prose, you can detect the miss and retry with a forced
tool_choice
. Use this sparingly — if the model consistently skips a tool, fix the description rather than patching with force:

# Pattern: handle the case where the model calls no tool but you expected one

def ensure_tool_called(user_msg: str, required_tool: str, tools: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    Make a first attempt with auto tool_choice.
    If no tool was called, retry with tool_choice forced.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=512,
        tools=tools, tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}]
    )

    called = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
    if called:
        return response

    # Model skipped the tool — force it on a second attempt
    forced = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=512,
        tools=tools,
        tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": required_tool},
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}]
    )
    return forced
Force-retry is a last resort — rely on it means the description needs work
Log every forced retry so you can identify which tools need description improvement
Never force-retry more than once per turn — two misses signals a systemic problem
Forced calls still produce the model's best guess at arguments — validate them before executing
Scaling to Large Tool Sets

When you have many tools (> 10–15), presenting all of them at once makes selection harder and increases latency. A two-stage approach — first select a category tool, then call domain tools — keeps each selection decision small:

The category tools are lightweight meta-tools with no real implementation — they just return a confirmation string. Their only purpose is to route the model to the right domain tool set. This pattern reduces the effective selection problem from N tools to category_count + domain_count per stage.
Summary

The model selects tools by matching each description's 'when to use' clause to the user's intent. Explicit positive triggers ('Use for X'), negative triggers ('Do NOT use for Y'), and comparison clauses ('Use this instead of Z when...') produce reliable selection. Overlapping tools need explicit disambiguation. System-prompt policies add a rule layer on top of description-based selection. Dynamic tool lists reduce noise by only offering tools relevant to the current context.
